channel.title: Comments for page "Perfect Mind"
channel.description: Posts in the discussion thread "Perfect Mind"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Your take on this tale is simply brilliant. Thank you for your thoughtful approach, this line of thought is approximately what I wanted to invoke with this tale.

I really like your idea of a "corrupted" nirvana. Really, a person who has experienced Satori feels one with the world. In this case, that person was enlightened, but in some perverted sense. Instead of becoming aware of their own illusory nature and becoming one with everything, this person became aware of their own illusory nature and dragged the universe after him/herself. You were right to note that the person is concentrated on the idea of a self. My translator pointed it out to me that in the end of the tale this person repeatedly says "I… I… I…" - seems like instead of "dissolving" their own consciousness they made their ego absolute and "dissolved" the world in it. I may assume that person was a latent reality bender - how else would they be able to expand their internal state to the real outside world?

The idea with preta is actually interesting. I think I red something about them in the Tibetan book of the Dead. But, as far as I understand, these creatures are obsessed with their own sansaric desires and passions, whereas the person in my tale rejected all of this. If one was to continue drawing Buddhist analogies, the person realized the world is but a ripple on water and tried to return the world to its true state - a smooth and level water surface.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So even with the "revelation" that his mentor gave him, this person remained delusional to the end, thinking that eliminating his or her perception of the objects was the same thing as eliminating the object itself. That puts a lot of perspective into this tale.

Am I correct in guessing that this person was somebody who almost reached Buddhist nirvana during his final steps, but missed the mark completely? He seems to have been a latent reality warper, which would not have been helpful for his experience. Furthermore, it looks like the mentor was trying to give him a shortcut to nirvana. Without the knowledge and the understanding of the path leading to nirvana, he or she messes it up completely by holding on to the idea of a "self;" hence, an aggregate of senses, experiences, and vague motives as well as the cognitive process, as you described it, still remains.

Are you very familiar with Buddhist cosmology and rebirth? Because this interpretation of SCP-2470 matches that of a preta, or a hungry ghost. A being that is forever craving the material world around it, seeking to satisfy its hunger, but here SCP-2470 is taken to a level where it poses a very real and dangerous threat, and is pulling pieces of reality into itself in order to sustain its insatiable craving. It's fitting too, since the person focused so much on emptiness and his or her last thoughts before death were about enforcing this view of emptiness onto the world. At least that's my interpretation of it.

The thing I loved about this piece is that even though it presents an interpretation of SCP-2470's origin, there's still many inferences that you can make about this story. This story has been lingering around in my head for days now. Seriously, this was a very cool and interesting piece to read.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That person only "erased" things they directly percieved. Fortunately, they collapsed before they realized that they can destroy all the world. But the cognitive process in SCP-2470 slowly goes on, so theoretically it can do that in the future.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Woah. Since the person in this tale (SCP-2470) was able to empty everything, including the sky, the stars, the dirt, and finally himself, does this tale depict a scenario where SCP-2470 is completely successful?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked it, even if I didn't know the skip it involves. Gave me a a Kubrickian1 vibe. (And whoa I just realized you used an image. Great job on the translations guys! +1

Footnotes
1. is that a real word?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not a member of the Image Team but I have my doubts that this article meets mainsite Image use Policy. So you should probably double-check with them.

The Mind palace:
Google reverse image search

Which ultimately led me to:
http://dghg.bake-neko.net/ewr_en.html

The universe:
http://reflectionsonwire.blogspot.com/2015_04_01_archive.html

This is a still from the 'Jupiter and Beyond' section of the Stanley Kubrick film '2001'.

The room:
http://stokpic.com/project/retro-office-space-with-books-furniture-and-sun-flare/

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dumb question, but should the tale title be Perfect Mind?

No problem! Fixed.

Happy to hear you like it. By the way, I would like to mention that this take on 2470 differs from the author's original idea. According to theTBAPb's design 2470 isn't (and wasn't) a sapient being, but emptiness itself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dumb question, but should the tale title be Perfect Mind? (It's more or less a non-issue in any case!)

I really enjoy this. Unique perspective on 2470 that I hadn't considered before that really adds to that skip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks to Gene R for the help with translation.
If you feel our English is incorrect, we'd be grateful if you would point that out.
